div.title: Chapter 1
div.verse: In the beginning God created the heaven and the earth.The Hebrew word translated "beginning" (bərēʾšīt) indicates an absolute beginning of time and matter.
And the earth was without form, and void; and darkness was upon the face of the deep. And the Spirit of God moved upon the face of the waters.See John 1:1–3; Heb 11:3.
And God said, Let there be light: and there was light.
And God saw the light, that it was good: and God divided the light from the darkness.

And God called the light Day, and the darkness he called Night. And the evening and the morning were the first day.The Hebrew word yōm ("day") here follows the pattern evening-then-morning, reflecting the Jewish reckoning of a day from sunset to sunset.

And God said, Let us make man in our image, after our likeness: and let them have dominion over the fish of the sea, and over the fowl of the air, and over the cattle, and over all the earth, and over every creeping thing that creepeth upon the earth.The plural "us" has been variously interpreted as a reference to the divine council, the Trinity, or a majestic plural (pluralis majestatis).
So God created man in his own image, in the image of God created he him; male and female created he them.
And God saw every thing that he had made, and, behold, it was very good. And the evening and the morning were the sixth day.

div.title: Chapter 2
div.verse: Thus the heavens and the earth were finished, and all the host of them.
And on the seventh day God ended his work which he had made; and he rested on the seventh day from all his work which he had made.Cf. Heb 4:4; Ex 20:11.
And God blessed the seventh day, and sanctified it: because that in it he had rested from all his work which God created and made.

And the Lord God formed man of the dust of the ground, and breathed into his nostrils the breath of life; and man became a living soul.The word translated "soul" (nep̄eš) refers to the whole living being, not merely a separable inner essence.
And the Lord God took the man, and put him into the garden of Eden to dress it and to keep it.
Therefore shall a man leave his father and his mother, and shall cleave unto his wife: and they shall be one flesh.Quoted in Matt 19:5; Mark 10:7–8; Eph 5:31.

div.title: Chapter 3
div.verse: Now the serpent was more subtil than any beast of the field which the Lord God had made. And he said unto the woman, Yea, hath God said, Ye shall not eat of every tree of the garden?The serpent (Hebrew nāḥāš) is identified in Rev 12:9 and 20:2 with Satan.

And when the woman saw that the tree was good for food, and that it was pleasant to the eyes, and a tree to be desired to make one wise, she took of the fruit thereof, and did eat, and gave also unto her husband with her; and he did eat.

And I will put enmity between thee and the woman, and between thy seed and her seed; it shall bruise thy head, and thou shalt bruise his heel.This verse is traditionally called the protoevangelium ("first gospel"), seen as a prophecy of Christ's victory over Satan.
So he drove out the man; and he placed at the east of the garden of Eden Cherubims, and a flaming sword which turned every way, to keep the way of the tree of life.
